good_side: A browser-based workflow is easy to demo, iterate, and deploy.
bad_side: The project can sprawl into auth, dashboards, collaboration, and design polish before the core loop works.
core_proof: A user can complete the primary workflow in the browser and see a useful result.
core_workflow: Capture input, process it, persist or display the result, and let the user act on it.
constraints: Prefer static HTML/CSS/vanilla JS unless a framework is justified
Keep v1 to one core workflow
Make the working surface domain-specific and fast
workflow_steps: Open the app and provide the main input
Review or edit the generated result
Save, export, or complete the result
entity_purpose: Stores the primary thing the user creates or manages
relationships: Owned by the current user or local workspace
risk: The UI looks complete before the workflow is actually useful.
mitigation: Build the end-to-end workflow before secondary screens.
open_question: Does v1 need accounts, or can it run as a local/single-user app first?
later_task: Add account-based persistence if the first workflow proves useful.
core_assertion: The core workflow produces the expected result from realistic input.
acceptance: Expected output appears and persists after refresh if persistence exists

stack.FRONTEND: Next.js or the repo's existing frontend framework
stack.STORAGE: Start with local/file/mock storage unless persistence is core
stack.INTEGRATIONS: Only integrations needed by the core workflow
stack.DEPLOYMENT_TARGET: Vercel or the repo's existing host
